name: JSON Web Token
description: JSON Web Token (JWT) is a compact, stateless token format used to securely transmit claims between an identity provider and service endpoints. It encodes header, payload, and signature data in a URL-safe manner, enabling scalable, decentralized authentication and authorization without server-side session storage. Security hinges on robust signing/encryption algorithms, strict claim and header validation, secure key management, enforced expiration, and protected transport and storage. Improper configuration or implementation flaws can expose JWTs to signature bypass, weak-key attacks, token tampering, replay, theft, or unauthorized access.

name: Customer account data
description: Customer account data is a core data entity containing personally identifiable information (PII), account identifiers, transaction history, authentication-related metadata, and interaction logs used to manage customer relationships and business workflows. As a data asset it is created, read, updated, and deleted by multiple services (web/mobile front-ends, APIs, databases, analytics pipelines, backup systems and third-party integrations). Security considerations include confidentiality, integrity and availability of stored records; access control boundaries between applications, services and privileged administrators; encryption at rest and in transit; retention and deletion policies; and audit and monitoring requirements to detect misuse. Integration points and dependencies commonly include identity providers, payment processors, analytics/CRM systems, backup/storage services and vendor connectors. No inherited class information was provided.

name: SES Message Payload
description: The SES Message Payload defines the structure and content of an email sent via Amazon SES. It encapsulates sender and recipient addresses (To, Cc, Bcc), email body formats (plain text, HTML, raw MIME, or templates), optional reply-to addresses, configuration set identifiers for tracking, and custom tags. The payload is assembled by application logic, transmitted over SES APIs, and may include sensitive data or dynamic content. Security considerations include proper input validation to prevent injection, encryption of payload in transit and at rest, strict IAM policies to restrict API access, monitoring of payload logs to avoid data leakage, and validation of template sources to mitigate supply-chain risks.

name: User Credentials
description: User Credentials represent secret authentication data including usernames and passwords used to verify user identities and grant access to services. These credentials are processed, transmitted, and stored by authentication systems and must be protected across their lifecycle. Weaknesses in storage, hashing, transmission, or management can lead to unauthorized access, identity theft, and system compromise. Credentials integrate with identity providers, authentication modules, and authorization services and often depend on secure hashing algorithms, encryption, and multi-factor authentication.

name: Telemetry logs
description: The Telemetry logs component serves as a persistent storage repository for telemetry messages exchanged between systems (e.g., autopilot and ground station). It ingests, indexes, and retains time-series records of system performance and status, enabling monitoring, troubleshooting, and post-flight analysis. As a data store, it exposes APIs or file interfaces for log ingestion and retrieval, enforces access controls, and may integrate with analytics or alerting systems. It forms a trust boundary between operational devices and analysis tools, requiring secure handling to prevent unauthorized access, tampering, or leakage of sensitive operational data.

name: Telemetry metrics
description: The Telemetry metrics data entity represents operational and performance data points collected from applications or devices in real-time. It is generated by telemetry agents, transported over network channels, and stored in monitoring backends for analysis and visualization. The data entity may include performance counters, usage statistics, error logs, and system health indicators. Telemetry metrics integrate with data pipelines, dashboards, and alerting systems, forming a critical part of observability. Security implications arise from potential exposure of internal system behaviors, sensitive configuration details, and trends that could reveal vulnerabilities or user patterns. Proper handling requires encryption in transit and at rest, strict access controls, and integrity protection to ensure the fidelity and confidentiality of monitoring data.
